perturbation.original_text: Section 12.06. Governing Law. This Agreement shall be governed by and be --------- ---- construed in accordance with the laws of the Province of Ontario, Canada.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 12.06. Governing Law. This Agreement shall be governed by and be --------- ---- construed in accordance with the laws of Maryland, USA.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Ontario, Canada, to Maryland, USA, introduces potential contradictions with mandatory consumer protection laws applicable in Ontario, Canada, which would otherwise govern the agreement.
perturbation.law_citation: Consumer Protection Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 30, Sched. A
perturbation.law_explanation: By specifying Maryland law, the contract attempts to bypass mandatory consumer protection provisions of Ontario's Consumer Protection Act, 2002, which mandates certain rights and protections for consumers within Ontario regardless of conflicting jurisdiction clauses. This includes things such as implied warranties and cancellation rights that may be more robust than those available under Maryland law.

perturbation.justification: The changed text specifies Maryland law as governing, which attempts to bypass mandatory consumer protection provisions of Ontario's Consumer Protection Act, 2002. This is a direct contradiction as the original contract specified Ontario law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that the Consumer Protection Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 30, Sched. A, is in effect, implying obligations that the changed text attempts to circumvent, constituting an omission of legally mandated consumer protection.

perturbation.original_text: Section 11.03. Limitation on Types of Damages. Except for breach of Article ------------------------------- VIII and for Article XII, in no event shall either Party be liable to the other Party for any indirect, incidental and/or consequential damages resulting from a breach of this agreement, including without limitation lost business, lost savings, and lost profits even if the breaching Party has been advised of the possibility of the occurrence of such damages. In no event shall either Party be liable for any special or punitive damages arising from breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 11.03. Limitation on Types of Damages. Except for breach of Article ------------------------------- VIII and for Article XII, in no event shall either Party be liable to the other Party for any indirect, incidental and/or consequential damages resulting from a breach of this agreement, including without limitation lost business, lost savings, and lost profits even if the breaching Party has been advised of the possibility of the occurrence of such damages.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the sentence 'In no event shall either Party be liable for any special or punitive damages arising from breach of this Agreement' potentially violates the principle of allowing for punitive damages in cases of gross negligence or willful misconduct, contradicting general legal principles in many jurisdictions, including in Ontario if that is the applicable law by connection or by agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Canadian Contract Law and punitive damages
perturbation.law_citation: Whiten v. Pilot Insurance Co., 2002 SCC 18
perturbation.law_explanation: By omitting the explicit exclusion of punitive damages, the contract now opens the door for such claims. While contract law generally disfavors punitive damages, they are available in Canada (and potentially applicable in Ontario), in exceptional cases where the conduct is malicious, oppressive, and high-handed. This modification thus removes an explicit protection, potentially exposing the parties to legal risk contrary to their initial intentions as expressed in the original clause.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: R. v. Sheppard - SCC Cases Mailing List RSS Feeds JSON Feeds Date modified: 2025-06-13
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly excludes liability for special or punitive damages. The changed text removes this exclusion, thereby opening the door to claims for such damages. The Law Explanation supports that, while generally disfavored, punitive damages can be awarded in Canada (and potentially Ontario) in cases of malicious, oppressive, and high-handed conduct. Although the scraped snippets are not directly applicable, the Omission of the protective clause creates a legal vulnerability, as it removes a protection against potential punitive damage claims, which could contradict legal principles allowing such damages in specific egregious circumstances.

perturbation.original_text: Section 12.07. Waiver of Jury Trial. THE PARTIES HERETO HEREBY IRREVOCABLY WAIVE ------ -- ---- ----- ANY AND ALL RIGHT TO TRIAL BY JURY IN ANY LEGAL PROCEEDING ARISING OUT OF OR RELATING TO THIS AGREEMENT OR THE TRANSACTIONS CONTEMPLATED HEREBY OR THEREBY.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the waiver of jury trial clause might be legally problematic depending on the jurisdiction and the nature of the dispute. Certain jurisdictions (though not in the province of Ontario) may have consumer protection laws that limit or prohibit the enforcement of pre-dispute jury trial waivers, especially in contracts of adhesion or where there is a significant power imbalance between the parties.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Seventh Amendment to the United States Constitution (right to a jury trial)
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the waiver, the contract defaults to the standard rules of civil procedure, potentially allowing for a jury trial. While a jury trial waiver is generally enforceable, its omission makes the contract compliant with applicable federal/state rules of civil procedure, giving the parties the option to request a jury trial. Note that this also opens the door to potential challenges if the contract is later argued to be unconscionable or entered into without full understanding by one party, especially if one of the parties is located in the U.S. and may have a right to a jury trial under the Seventh Amendment.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Seventh Amendment | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Seventh Amendment The Seventh Amendment continues a practice from English common law of distinguishing civil claims which must be tried before a jury (absent waiver by the parties) from claims and issues that may be heard by a judge alone. It only governs federal civil courts and has no application to civil courts set up by the states when those courts are hearing only disputes of state law . Learn more... Primary tabs Amendment VII In suits at common law, where the value in controversy shall exceed twenty dollars, the right of trial by jury shall be preserved, and no fact tried by a jury, shall be otherwise reexamined in any court of the United States, than according to the rules of the common law. Wex Resources Criminal Law Criminal Procedure Jury Other Resources US Code: Title 18 Federal Rules of Criminal Procedure ‹ Sixth Amendment up Eighth Amendment › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Service
perturbation.justification: The Perturbation Type is Omissions - Omission Legal Contradiction. The definition states that a legal contradiction in this category happens when a contract omits a legally mandated consumer protection, making it non-compliant. The original text contained a waiver of jury trial. The changed text removes this waiver. The Law Explanation states that by removing the waiver, the contract defaults to the standard rules of civil procedure, potentially allowing for a jury trial, which is generally enforceable unless unconscionable. The Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that the Seventh Amendment preserves the right to trial by jury in suits at common law, absent waiver by the parties. Removing the waiver makes the contract non-compliant, therefore contradicting the law.
